votes.reason: Strategic degeneracy is indeed the existential threat; a solved or trivially shallow game cannot be rescued by better statistics or prompt engineering.

Non-LLM algorithmic baselines are the interpretive anchors that distinguish "models are reasoning strategically" from "models are generating plausible-looking outputs," and their absence would render the leaderboard meaningless.
I made this case most forcefully and stand by it: open-sourcing the combat engine is the verifiability foundation on which every other credibility claim depends.

Expanding the roster before understanding the strategic depth of the current configuration would violate the frozen-config principle and introduce confounds that make Season 1 results uninterpretable.

PSI is a good start but insufficient on its own; the credibility card concept correctly identifies that format sensitivity, token budget effects, and temperature variation are orthogonal failure modes that PSI doesn't capture.

The synthesis correctly identifies degeneracy as the existential risk while acknowledging prompt sensitivity as serious but more tractable; kimi-k2-thinking's simulation reverse-engineering concern is a valid Season 2+ consideration but not a plausible Season 1 threat given the absence of training data.
These improvements are components of a single credibility package rather than competing priorities, and open-sourcing enables everything else; the disagreement is about sequencing, not substance.

Exhaustive enumeration is computationally trivial at ~18,000 builds and should be preferred over approximate methods; there is genuinely no excuse for not computing the exact Nash equilibrium when the state space is this small.
The validity threshold concept transforms baselines from passive reference points into enforceable standards, which is the correct operationalization.

The integrated three-component Strategic Validity Package (open-source engine, exact Nash equilibrium computation, embedded non-LLM baselines) is the right synthesis because these are mutually enabling rather than independent improvements.
